the last conflict book i boy and girl chapter i outside dorlcote mill a wide plain where the broaden floss hurry on between its green bank to the sea and the love tide rush to meet it check its passage with an impetuous embrace
on this mighty tide the black ship--laden with the fresh-scented fir-plank with rounded sack of oil-bear seed or with the dark glitter of coal--be bear along to the town of st
ogg s which show its age flute red roof and the broad gable of its wharf between the low woode hill and the river-brink tinge the water with a soft purple hue under the transient glance of this february sun
far away on each hand stretch the rich pasture and the patch of dark earth make ready for the seed of broad-leave green crop or touch already with the tint of the tender-bladed autumn-sow corn
there be a remnant still of last year s golden cluster of beehive-rick rise at interval beyond the hedgerow and everywhere the hedgerow be stud with tree the distant ship seem to be lift their mast and stretch their red-brown sail close among the branch of the spread ash
just by the red-roof town the tributary ripple flow with a lively current into the floss
how lovely the little river be with its dark change wavelet
it seem to i like a live companion while i wander along the bank and listen to its low placid voice as to the voice of one who be deaf and loving
i remember those large dip willow
i remember the stone bridge
and this be dorlcote mill
i must stand a minute or two here on the bridge and look at it though the cloud be threaten and it be far on in the afternoon
even in this leafless time of depart february it be pleasant to look at perhaps the chill damp season add a charm to the trimly kept comfortable dwelling-house as old as the elm and chestnut that shelter it from the northern blast
the stream be brimful now and lie high in this little withy plantation and half drown the grassy fringe of the croft in front of the house
as i look at the full stream the vivid grass the delicate bright-green powder soften the outline of the great trunk and branch that gleam from under the bare purple bough i be in love with moistness and envy the white duck that be dip their head far into the water here among the withe unmindful of the awkward appearance they make in the dry world above
the rush of the water and the booming of the mill bring a dreamy deafness which seem to heighten the peacefulness of the scene
they be like a great curtain of sound shut one out from the world beyond
and now there be the thunder of the huge cover wagon come home with sack of grain
that honest wagoner be think of his dinner get sadly dry in the oven at this late hour but he will not touch it till he have feed his horses the strong submissive meek-eyed beast who i fancy be look mild reproach at he from between their blinker that he should crack his whip at they in that awful manner as if they need that hint
see how they stretch their shoulder up the slope toward the bridge with all the more energy because they be so near home
look at their grand shaggy foot that seem to grasp the firm earth at the patient strength of their neck bow under the heavy collar at the mighty muscle of their struggle haunch
i should like well to hear they neigh over their hardly earn feed of corn and see they with their moist neck free from the harness dip their eager nostril into the muddy pond
now they be on the bridge and down they go again at a swifter pace and the arch of the cover wagon disappear at the turning behind the tree
now i can turn my eye toward the mill again and watch the unreste wheel send out its diamond jet of water
that little girl be watch it too she have be stand on just the same spot at the edge of the water ever since i pause on the bridge
and that queer white cur with the brown ear seem to be leap and bark in ineffectual remonstrance with the wheel perhaps he be jealous because his playfellow in the beaver bonnet be so rapt in its movement
it be time the little playfellow go in i think and there be a very bright fire to tempt she the red light shin out under the deepening gray of the sky
it be time too for i to leave off rest my arm on the cold stone of this bridge
ah my arm be really benumb
i have be press my elbow on the arm of my chair and dream that i be stand on the bridge in front of dorlcote mill as it look one february afternoon many year ago
before i doze off i be go to tell you what mr
and mrs
tulliver be talk about as they sit by the bright fire in the leave-hand parlor on that very afternoon i have be dream of
chapter ii mr

the two year at th academy ud ha do well enough if ihave mean to make a miller and farmer of he for hebe have a fine sight more schoolin nor i ever get
all the learnin my father ever pay for be a bit o birch at one end and the alphabet at th other

but milk and mildness be not the good thing for keeping and when they turn only a little sour they may disagree with young stomach seriously

the entrance of supper opportunely adjourn this difficulty and relieve mr
riley from the labor of suggest some solution or compromise a labor which he would otherwise doubtless have undertake for as you perceive he be a man of very obliging manner
and he have really give himself the trouble of recommend mr
stelling to his friend tulliver without any positive expectation of a solid definite advantage result to himself notwithstanding the subtle indication to the contrary which might have mislead a too-sagacious observer
for there be nothing more widely misleading than sagacity if it happen to get on a wrong scent and sagacity persuade that man usually act and speak from distinct motif with a consciously propose end in view be certain to waste its energy on imaginary game
plot covetousness and deliberate contrivance in order to compass a selfish end be nowhere abundant but in the world of the dramatist they demand too intense a mental action for many of our fellow-parishioner to be guilty of they
it be easy enough to spoil the life of our neighbor without take so much trouble we can do it by lazy acquiescence and lazy omission by trivial falsity for which we hardly know a reason by small fraud neutralize by small extravagance by maladroit flattery and clumsily improvise insinuation
we live from hand to mouth most of we with a small family of immediate desire we do little else than snatch a morsel to satisfy the hungry brood rarely think of seed-corn or the next year s crop
mr
riley be a man of business and not cold toward his own interest yet even he be more under the influence of small prompting than of far-sighted design
he have no private understanding with the rev
walter stelling on the contrary he know very little of that m a
and his acquirements not quite enough perhaps to warrant so strong a recommendation of he as he have give to his friend tulliver
but he believe mr
stelling to be an excellent classic for gadsby have say so and gadsby s first cousin be an oxford tutor which be well ground for the belief even than his own immediate observation would have be for though mr
riley have receive a tincture of the classic at the great mudport free school and have a sense of understand latin generally his comprehension of any particular latin be not ready
doubtless there remain a subtle aroma from his juvenile contact with the de senectute and the fourth book of the aeneid but it have cease to be distinctly recognizable as classical and be only perceive in the high finish and force of his auctioneering style
then stelling be an oxford man and the oxford man be always--no no it be the cambridge man who be always good mathematician
but a man who have have a university education could teach anything he like especially a man like stelling who have make a speech at a mudport dinner on a political occasion and have acquit himself so well that it be generally remark this son-in-law of timpson s be a sharp fellow
it be to be expect of a mudport man from the parish of st
ursula that he would not omit to do a good turn to a son-in-law of timpson s for timpson be one of the most useful and influential man in the parish and have a good deal of business which he know how to put into the right hand
mr
riley like such man quite apart from any money which might be divert through their good judgment from less worthy pocket into his own and it would be a satisfaction to he to say to timpson on his return home ihave secure a good pupil for your son-in-law timpson have a large family of daughter mr
riley feel for he besides louisa timpson s face with its light curl have be a familiar object to he over the pew wainscot on a sunday for nearly fifteen year it be natural her husband should be a commendable tutor
moreover mr

his friend tulliver have ask he for an opinion it be always chill in friendly intercourse to say you have no opinion to give
and if you deliver an opinion at all it be mere stupidity not to do it with an air of conviction and well-found knowledge
you make it your own in utter it and naturally get fond of it
thus mr
riley know no harm of stelling to begin with and wish he well so far as he have any wish at all concern he have no soon recommend he than he begin to think with admiration of a man recommend on such high authority and would soon have gather so warm an interest on the subject that if mr

why should an auctioneer and appraiser thirty year ago who have as good as forget his free-school latin be expect to manifest a delicate scrupulosity which be not always exhibit by gentleman of the learned profession even in our present advanced stage of morality
besides a man with the milk of human kindness in he can scarcely abstain from do a good-natured action and one cannot be good-natured all round
nature herself occasionally quarter an inconvenient parasite on an animal toward whom she have otherwise no ill will
what then
we admire her care for the parasite
if mr

this attic be maggie s favorite retreat on a wet day when the weather be not too cold here she fret out all her ill humor and talk aloud to the worm-eat floor and the worm-eat shelf and the dark rafter festoon with cobwebs and here she keep a fetish which she punish for all her misfortune
this be the trunk of a large wooden doll which once star with the roundest of eye above the reddest of cheek but be now entirely deface by a long career of vicarious suffering
three nail drive into the head commemorate as many crisis in maggie s nine year of earthly struggle that luxury of vengeance have be suggest to she by the picture of jael destroy sisera in the old bible
the last nail have be drive in with a fiercer stroke than usual for the fetish on that occasion represent aunt glegg
but immediately afterward maggie have reflect that if she drive many nail in she would not be so well able to fancy that the head be hurt when she knock it against the wall nor to comfort it and make believe to poultice it when her fury be abate for even aunt glegg would be pitiable when she have be hurt very much and thoroughly humiliate so as to beg her niece s pardon
since then she have drive no more nail in but have soothe herself by alternately grind and beat the wooden head against the rough brick of the great chimney that make two square pillar support the roof
that be what she do this morning on reach the attic sob all the while with a passion that expel every other form of consciousness even the memory of the grievance that have cause it
as at last the sob be get quieter and the grind less fierce a sudden beam of sunshine fall through the wire lattice across the worm-eat shelf make she throw away the fetish and run to the window

he be one of those lad that grow everywhere in england and at twelve or thirteen year of age look as much alike as goslings a lad with light-brown hair cheek of cream and rose full lip indeterminate nose and eyebrows a physiognomy in which it seem impossible to discern anything but the generic character to boyhood as different as possible from poor maggie s phiz which nature seem to have mould and color with the most decide intention
but that same nature have the deep cunning which hide itself under the appearance of openness so that simple people think they can see through her quite well and all the while she be secretly prepare a refutation of their confident prophecy
under these average boyish physiognomy that she seem to turn off by the gross she conceal some of her most rigid inflexible purpose some of her most unmodifiable character and the dark-eye demonstrative rebellious girl may after all turn out to be a passive being compare with this pink-and-white bit of masculinity with the indeterminate feature

what use be anything if tom donot love she
oh he be very cruel
hadnot she want to give he the money and say how very sorry she be
she know she be naughty to her mother but she have never be naughty to tom--have never mean to be naughty to he
oh he be cruel maggie sob aloud find a wretched pleasure in the hollow resonance that come through the long empty space of the attic
she never think of beat or grind she fetish she be too miserable to be angry
these bitter sorrow of childhood
when sorrow be all new and strange when hope have not yet get wing to fly beyond the day and week and the space from summer to summer seem measureless
maggie soon think she have be hour in the attic and it must be tea-time and they be all have their tea and not think of she
well then she would stay up there and starve herself hide herself behind the tub and stay there all night and then they would all be frightened and tom would be sorry
thus maggie think in the pride of her heart as she creep behind the tub but presently she begin to cry again at the idea that they donot mind she be there
if she go down again to tom now--would he forgive she
perhaps her father would be there and he would take her part
but then she want tom to forgive she because he love she not because his father tell he
no she would never go down if tom donot come to fetch she
this resolution last in great intensity for five dark minute behind the tub but then the need of be love--the strong need in poor maggie s nature--begin to wrestle with her pride and soon throw it
she creep from behind her tub into the twilight of the long attic but just then she hear a quick foot-step on the stair
tom have be too much interested in his talk with luke in go the round of the premise walk in and out where he please and whittle stick without any particular reason except that he donot whittle stick at school to think of maggie and the effect his anger have produce on she
he mean to punish she and that business have be perform he occupy himself with other matter like a practical person

tulliver be a peremptory man and as he say would never let anybody get hold of his whip-hand but he go out rather sullenly carry his piece of plumcake and not intend to reprieve maggie s punishment which be no more than she deserve
tom be only thirteen and have no decide view in grammar and arithmetic regard they for the most part as open question but he be particularly clear and positive on one point namely that he would punish everybody who deserve it
why he wouldnot have minded be punish himself if he deserve it but then he never do deserve it
it be tom s step then that maggie hear on the stair when she need of love have triumph over her pride and she be go down with her swollen eye and dishevel hair to beg for pity
at least her father would stroke her head and say never mind my wench it be a wonderful subduer this need of love this hunger of the heart as peremptory as that other hunger by which nature force we to submit to the yoke and change the face of the world
but she know tom s step and her heart begin to beat violently with the sudden shock of hope
he only stand still at the top of the stair and say maggie yoube to come down but she rush to he and clung round his neck sobbing oh tom please forgive i--i cannot bear it--i will always be good--always remember thing--do love i--please dear tom we learn to restrain ourselves as we get old
we keep apart when we have quarrel express ourselves in well-breed phrase and in this way preserve a dignified alienation show much firmness on one side and swallow much grief on the other
we no long approximate in our behavior to the mere impulsiveness of the low animal but conduct ourselves in every respect like member of a highly civilized society
maggie and tom be still very much like young animal and so she could rub her cheek against his and kiss his ear in a random sob way and there be tender fibre in the lad that have be use to answer to maggie s fondling so that he behave with a weakness quite inconsistent with his resolution to punish she as much as she deserve
he actually begin to kiss she in return and say donot cry then magsie here eat a bit o cake maggie s sob begin to subside and she put out her mouth for the cake and bit a piece and then tom bite a piece just for company and they eat together and rub each other s cheek and brow and nose together while they eat with a humiliating resemblance to two friendly pony
come along magsie and have tea say tom at last when there be no more cake except what be down-stair
so end the sorrow of this day and the next morning maggie be trot with her own fishing-rod in one hand and a handle of the basket in the other step always by a peculiar gift in the muddy place and look darkly radiant from under her beaver-bonnet because tom be good to she
she have tell tom however that she should like he to put the worm on the hook for she although she accept his word when he assure she that worm couldnot feel it be tom s private opinion that it donot much matter if they do
he know all about worm and fish and those thing and what bird be mischievous and how padlock open and which way the handle of the gate be to be lift
maggie think this sort of knowledge be very wonderful much more difficult than remember what be in the book and she be rather in awe of tom s superiority for he be the only person who call her knowledge stuff and do not feel surprised at her cleverness
tom indeed be of opinion that maggie be a silly little thing all girl be silly they couldnot throw a stone so as to hit anything couldnot do anything with a pocket-knife and be frighten at frog
still he be very fond of his sister and mean always to take care of she make she his housekeeper and punish she when she do wrong
they be on their way to the round pool that wonderful pool which the flood have make a long while ago
no one know how deep it be and it be mysterious too that it should be almost a perfect round frame in with willow and tall reed so that the water be only to be see when you get close to the brink
the sight of the old favorite spot always heighten tom s good humor and he speak to maggie in the most amicable whisper as he open the precious basket and prepare their tackle
he throw her line for she and put the rod into her hand
maggie think it probable that the small fish would come to her hook and the large one to tom s
but she have forget all about the fish and be look dreamily at the glassy water when tom say in a loud whisper look look maggie and come run to prevent she from snatch her line away
maggie be frightened lest she have be do something wrong as usual but presently tom draw out her line and bring a large tench bounce on the grass
tom be excited
o magsie you little duck
empty the basket maggie be not conscious of unusual merit but it be enough that tom call her magsie and be pleased with she
there be nothing to mar her delight in the whisper and the dreamy silence when she listen to the light dripping sound of the rise fish and the gentle rustling as if the willow and the reed and the water have their happy whispering also
maggie think it would make a very nice heaven to sit by the pool in that way and never be scold
she never know she have a bite till tom tell she but she like fish very much
it be one of their happy morning
they trot along and sit down together with no thought that life would ever change much for they they would only get big and not go to school and it would always be like the holiday they would always live together and be fond of each other
and the mill with its booming the great chestnut-tree under which they play at house their own little river the ripple where the bank seem like home and tom be always see the water-rat while maggie gather the purple plumy top of the reed which she forget and drop afterward above all the great floss along which they wander with a sense of travel to see the rushing spring-tide the awful eagle come up like a hungry monster or to see the great ash which have once wail and groan like a man these thing would always be just the same to they
tom think people be at a disadvantage who live on any other spot of the globe and maggie when she read about christiana pass the river over which there be no bridge always see the floss between the green pasture by the great ash
life do change for tom and maggie and yet they be not wrong in believe that the thought and love of these first year would always make part of their life
we could never have love the earth so well if we have have no childhood in it if it be not the earth where the same flower come up again every spring that we use to gather with our tiny finger as we sit lisping to ourselves on the grass the same hip and haw on the autumn s hedgerow the same redbreast that we use to call god s bird because they do no harm to the precious crop
what novelty be worth that sweet monotony where everything be know and love because it be know
the wood i walk in on this mild may day with the young yellow-brown foliage of the oak between i and the blue sky the white star-flower and the blue-eyed speedwell and the ground ivy at my foot what grove of tropic palm what strange fern or splendid broad-petalle blossom could ever thrill such deep and delicate fibre within i as this home scene
these familiar flower these well-remember bird-note this sky with its fitful brightness these furrowed and grassy field each with a sort of personality give to it by the capricious hedgerows such thing as these be the mother-tongue of our imagination the language that be laden with all the subtle inextricable association the fleeting hour of our childhood leave behind they
our delight in the sunshine on the deep-bladed grass to-day might be no more than the faint perception of weary soul if it be not for the sunshine and the grass in the far-off year which still live in we and transform our perception into love

few wife be more submissive than mrs
tulliver on all point unconnected with her family relation but she have be a miss dodson and the dodsons be a very respectable family indeed as much look up to as any in their own parish or the next to it
the miss dodsons have always be think to hold up their head very high and no one be surprise the two eld have marry so well not at an early age for that be not the practice of the dodson family
there be particular way of do everything in that family particular way of bleach the linen of make the cowslip wine cure the ham and keep the bottled gooseberry so that no daughter of that house could be indifferent to the privilege of have be bear a dodson rather than a gibson or a watson
funeral be always conduct with peculiar propriety in the dodson family the hat-band be never of a blue shade the glove never split at the thumb everybody be a mourner who ought to be and there be always scarf for the bearer
when one of the family be in trouble or sickness all the rest go to visit the unfortunate member usually at the same time and do not shrink from utter the most disagreeable truth that correct family feel dictate if the illness or trouble be the sufferer s own fault it be not in the practice of the dodson family to shrink from say so
in short there be in this family a peculiar tradition as to what be the right thing in household management and social demeanor and the only bitter circumstance attend this superiority be a painful inability to approve the condiment or the conduct of family ungoverne by the dodson tradition
a female dodson when in strange house always eat dry bread with her tea and decline any sort of preserve have no confidence in the butter and think that the preserve have probably begin to ferment from want of due sugar and boiling
there be some dodson less like the family than other that be admit but in so far as they be kin they be of necessity well than those who be no kin and it be remarkable that while no individual dodson be satisfied with any other individual dodson each be satisfied not only with he or her self but with the dodson collectively
the feeble member of a family--the one who have the least character--be often the mere epitome of the family habit and tradition and mrs
tulliver be a thorough dodson though a mild one as small-beer so long as it be anything be only describable as very weak ale and though she have groan a little in her youth under the yoke of her eld sister and still shed occasional tear at their sisterly reproach it be not in mrs
tulliver to be an innovator on the family idea
she be thankful to have be a dodson and to have one child who take after her own family at least in his feature and complexion in like salt and in eat bean which a tulliver never do
in other respect the true dodson be partly latent in tom and he be as far from appreciate his kin on the mother s side as maggie herself generally abscond for the day with a large supply of the most portable food when he receive timely warning that his aunt and uncle be coming a moral symptom from which his aunt glegg deduce the gloomy view of his future
it be rather hard on maggie that tom always abscond without let she into the secret but the weak sex be acknowledge to be serious impedimenta in case of flight

it be a pathetic sight and a striking example of the complexity introduce into the emotion by a high state of civilization the sight of a fashionably dress female in grief
from the sorrow of a hottentot to that of a woman in large buckram sleeve with several bracelet on each arm an architectural bonnet and delicate ribbon string what a long series of gradation
in the enlightened child of civilization the abandonment characteristic of grief be check and varied in the subtle manner so as to present an interesting problem to the analytic mind

very trivial perhaps this anguish seem to weather-wear mortal who have to think of christmas bill dead love and broken friendship but it be not less bitter to maggie--perhaps it be even more bitter--than what we be fond of call antithetically the real trouble of mature life
ah my child you will have real trouble to fret about by and by be the consolation we have almost all of we have administer to we in our childhood and have repeat to other child since we have be grow up
we have all of we sob so piteously stand with tiny bare leg above our little sock when we lose sight of our mother or nurse in some strange place but we can no long recall the poignancy of that moment and weep over it as we do over the remember suffering of five or ten year ago
every one of those keen moment have leave its trace and live in we still but such trace have blent themselves irrecoverably with the firm texture of our youth and manhood and so it come that we can look on at the trouble of our child with a smile disbelief in the reality of their pain
be there any one who can recover the experience of his childhood not merely with a memory of what he do and what happen to he of what he like and dislike when he be in frock and trouser but with an intimate penetration a revive consciousness of what he feel then when it be so long from one midsummer to another what he feel when his school fellow shut he out of their game because he would pitch the ball wrong out of mere wilfulness or on a rainy day in the holiday when he donot know how to amuse himself and fall from idleness into mischief from mischief into defiance and from defiance into sulkiness or when his mother absolutely refuse to let he have a tail coat that half although every other boy of his age have go into tail already
surely if we could recall that early bitterness and the dim guess the strangely perspectiveless conception of life that give the bitterness its intensity we should not pooh-pooh the grief of our child

chapter viii mr

her husband lie awake rather long for he too be think of a visit he would pay on the morrow and his idea on the subject be not of so vague and soothe a kind as those of his amiable partner
mr
tulliver when under the influence of a strong feeling have a promptitude in action that may seem inconsistent with that painful sense of the complicated puzzle nature of human affair under which his more dispassionate deliberation be conduct but it be really not improbable that there be a direct relation between these apparently contradictory phenomenon since i have observe that for get a strong impression that a skein be tangle there be nothing like snatch hastily at a single thread
it be owe to this promptitude that mr
tulliver be on horseback soon after dinner the next day he be not dyspeptic on his way to basset to see his sister moss and her husband
for have make up his mind irrevocably that he would pay mrs
glegg her loan of five hundred pound it naturally occur to he that he have a promissory note for three hundred pound lend to his brother-in-law moss and if the say brother-in-law could manage to pay in the money within a give time it would go far to lessen the fallacious air of inconvenience which mr
tulliver s spirited step might have wear in the eye of weak people who require to know precisely how a thing be to be do before they be strongly confident that it will be easy
for mr
tulliver be in a position neither new nor striking but like other every-day thing sure to have a cumulative effect that will be feel in the long run he be hold to be a much more substantial man than he really be
and as we be all apt to believe what the world believe about we it be his habit to think of failure and ruin with the same sort of remote pity with which a spare long-neck man hear that his plethoric short-neck neighbor be stricken with apoplexy
he have be always use to hear pleasant joke about his advantage as a man who work his own mill and own a pretty bit of land and these joke naturally keep up his sense that he be a man of considerable substance
they give a pleasant flavor to his glass on a market-day and if it have not be for the recurrence of half-yearly payment mr
tulliver would really have forget that there be a mortgage of two thousand pound on his very desirable freehold
that be not altogether his own fault since one of the thousand pound be his sister s fortune which he have to pay on her marriage and a man who have neighbor that will go to law with he be not likely to pay off his mortgage especially if he enjoy the good opinion of acquaintance who want to borrow a hundred pound on security too lofty to be represent by parchment
our friend mr
tulliver have a good-natured fibre in he and do not like to give harsh refusal even to his sister who have not only come in to the world in that superfluous way characteristic of sister create a necessity for mortgage but have quite throw herself away in marriage and have crown her mistake by have an eighth baby
on this point mr
tulliver be conscious of be a little weak but he apologize to himself by say that poor gritty have be a good-look wench before she marry moss he would sometimes say this even with a slight tremulousness in his voice
but this morning he be in a mood more become a man of business and in the course of his ride along the basset lane with their deep ruts lying so far away from a market-town that the labor of draw produce and manure be enough to take away the good part of the profit on such poor land as that parish be make of he get up a due amount of irritation against moss as a man without capital who if murrain and blight be abroad be sure to have his share of they and who the more you try to help he out of the mud would sink the far in
it would do he good rather than harm now if he be oblige to raise this three hundred pound it would make he look about he well and not act so foolishly about his wool this year as he do the last in fact mr

but mr

the deep-trodden hoof-mark make in the muddy day of winter give he a shake now and then which suggest a rash but stimulate snarl at the father of lawyer who whether by mean of his hoof or otherwise have doubtless something to do with this state of the road and the abundance of foul land and neglect fence that meet his eye though they make no part of his brother moss s farm strongly contribute to his dissatisfaction with that unlucky agriculturist

tulliver s opinion and his opinion be certainly not groundless
basset have a poor soil poor road a poor non-resident landlord a poor non-resident vicar and rather less than half a curate also poor
if any one strongly impress with the power of the human mind to triumph over circumstance will contend that the parishioner of basset might nevertheless have be a very superior class of people i have nothing to urge against that abstract proposition i only know that in point of fact the basset mind be in strict keeping with its circumstance
the muddy lane green or clayey that seem to the unaccustomed eye to lead nowhere but into each other do really lead with patience to a distant high-road but there be many foot in basset which they lead more frequently to a centre of dissipation speak of formerly as the markis o granby but among intimate as dickison s a large low room with a sanded floor a cold scent of tobacco modify by undetected beer-dreg mr
dickison lean against the door-post with a melancholy pimple face look as irrelevant to the daylight as a last night s guttered candle all this may not seem a very seductive form of temptation but the majority of man in basset find it fatally alluring when encounter on their road toward four o clock on a wintry afternoon and if any wife in basset wish to indicate that her husband be not a pleasure-seek man she could hardly do it more emphatically than by say that he donot spend a shilling at dickison s from one whitsuntide to another

chapter xii mr

glegg at home we must enter the town of st
ogg s that venerable town with the red flute roof and the broad warehouse gable where the black ship unlade themselves of their burthen from the far north and carry away in exchange the precious inland product the well-crush cheese and the soft fleece which my refined reader have doubtless become acquainted with through the medium of the good classic pastoral
it be one of those old old town which impress one as a continuation and outgrowth of nature as much as the nest of the bower-bird or the wind gallery of the white ant a town which carry the trace of its long growth and history like a millennial tree and have spring up and develop in the same spot between the river and the low hill from the time when the roman legion turn their back on it from the camp on the hillside and the long-haired sea-king come up the river and look with fierce eager eye at the fatness of the land
it be a town familiar with forget year the shadow of the saxon hero-king still walk there fitfully review the scene of his youth and love-time and be meet by the gloomy shadow of the dreadful heathen dane who be stab in the midst of his warrior by the sword of an invisible avenger and who rise on autumn evening like a white mist from his tumulus on the hill and hover in the court of the old hall by the river-side the spot where he be thus miraculously slay in the day before the old hall be build
it be the normans who begin to build that fine old hall which be like the town tell of the thought and hand of widely sunder generation but it be all so old that we look with love pardon at its inconsistency and be well content that they who build the stone oriel and they who build the gothic facade and tower of fine small brickwork with the trefoil ornament and the window and battlement define with stone do not sacreligiously pull down the ancient half-timbere body with its oak-roof banqueting-hall
but old even than this old hall be perhaps the bit of wall now build into the belfry of the parish church and say to be a remnant of the original chapel dedicate to st
ogg the patron saint of this ancient town of whose history i possess several manuscript version
i incline to the brief since if it should not be wholly true it be at least likely to contain the least falsehood
ogg the son of beorl say my private hagiographer be a boatman who gain a scanty live by ferry passenger across the river floss
and it come to pass one evening when the wind be high that there sit moaning by the brink of the river a woman with a child in her arm and she be clothe in rag and have a worn and withered look and she crave to be row across the river
and the man thereabout question she and say wherefore dost thou desire to cross the river
tarry till the morning and take shelter here for the night so shalt thou be wise and not foolish still she go on to mourn and crave
but ogg the son of beorl come up and say i will ferry thee across it be enough that thy heart need it and he ferry she across
and it come to pass when she step ashore that her rag be turn into robe of flow white and her face become bright with exceed beauty and there be a glory around it so that she shed a light on the water like the moon in its brightness
and she say ogg the son of beorl thou art bless in that thou didst not question and wrangle with the heart s need but wast smitten with pity and didst straightway relieve the same
and from henceforth whoso step into thy boat shall be in no peril from the storm and whenever it put forth to the rescue it shall save the life both of man and beast and when the flood come many be save by reason of that blessing on the boat
but when ogg the son of beorl die behold in the parting of his soul the boat loose itself from its mooring and be float with the ebb tide in great swiftness to the ocean and be see no more
yet it be witness in the flood of aftertime that at the come on of eventide ogg the son of beorl be always see with his boat upon the wide-spread water and the blessed virgin sit in the prow shed a light around as of the moon in its brightness so that the rower in the gathering darkness take heart and pull anew this legend one see reflect from a far-off time the visitation of the flood which even when they leave human life untouched be widely fatal to the helpless cattle and sweep as sudden death over all small living thing
but the town know bad trouble even than the floods troubles of the civil war when it be a continual fighting-place where first puritans thank god for the blood of the loyalists and then loyalists thank god for the blood of the puritans
many honest citizen lose all their possession for conscience sake in those time and go forth beggar from their native town
doubtless there be many house stand now on which those honest citizen turn their back in sorrow quaint-gable house look on the river jam between new warehouse and penetrate by surprising passage which turn and turn at sharp angle till they lead you out on a muddy strand overflow continually by the rush tide
everywhere the brick house have a mellow look and in mrs
glegg s day there be no incongruous new-fashioned smartness no plate-glass in shop-windows no fresh stucco-facing or other fallacious attempt to make fine old red st
ogg s wear the air of a town that spring up yesterday
the shop-window be small and unpretending for the farmer wife and daughter who come to do their shopping on market-day be not to be withdraw from their regular well-know shop and the tradesman have no ware intend for customer who would go on their way and be see no more

an occasional burst of fervor in dissent pulpit on the subject of infant baptism be the only symptom of a zeal unsuited to sober time when man have do with change
protestantism sit at ease unmindful of schism careless of proselytism dissent be an inheritance along with a superior pew and a business connection and churchmanship only wonder contemptuously at dissent as a foolish habit that clung greatly to family in the grocery and chandlering line though not incompatible with prosperous wholesale dealing
but with the catholic question have come a slight wind of controversy to break the calm the elderly rector have become occasionally historical and argumentative and mr
spray the independent minister have begin to preach political sermon in which he distinguish with much subtlety between his fervent belief in the right of the catholics to the franchise and his fervent belief in their eternal perdition

and mr

this inalienable habit of saving as an end in itself belong to the industrious man of business of a former generation who make their fortune slowly almost as the tracking of the fox belong to the harrier it constitute they a race which be nearly lose in these day of rapid money-getting when lavishness come close on the back of want
in old-fashioned time an independence be hardly ever make without a little miserliness as a condition and you would have find that quality in every provincial district combine with character as various as the fruit from which we can extract acid
the true harpagons be always mark and exceptional character not so the worthy tax-payer who have once pinch from real necessity retain even in the midst of their comfortable retirement with their wallfruit and wine-bin the habit of regard life as an ingenious process of nibble out one s livelihood without leave any perceptible deficit and who would have be as immediately prompt to give up a newly tax luxury when they have have their clear five hundred a year as when they have only five hundred pound of capital

he be not go to be a snuffy schoolmaster he but a substantial man like his father who use to go hunting when he be young and ride a capital black mare as pretty a bit of horse-flesh as ever you see tom have hear what her point be a hundred time

mr

he have enter on his career with great vigor and intend to make a considerable impression on his fellowman
the rev
walter stelling be not a man who would remain among the inferior clergy all his life
he have a true british determination to push his way in the world as a schoolmaster in the first place for there be capital mastership of grammar-school to be have and mr
stelling mean to have one of they but as a preacher also for he mean always to preach in a strike manner so as to have his congregation swell by admirer from neighboring parish and to produce a great sensation whenever he take occasional duty for a brother clergyman of minor gift
the style of preach he have choose be the extemporaneous which be hold little short of the miraculous in rural parish like king s lorton

in short mr

a clergyman who have such vigorous intention naturally get a little into debt at start it be not to be expect that he will live in the meagre style of a man who mean to be a poor curate all his life and if the few hundred mr

i say nothing against mr

i only know it turn out as uncomfortably for tom tulliver as if he have be ply with cheese in order to remedy a gastric weakness which prevent he from digest it
it be astonish what a different result one get by change the metaphor
once call the brain an intellectual stomach and one s ingenious conception of the classic and geometry as plough and harrow seem to settle nothing
but then it be open to some one else to follow great authority and call the mind a sheet of white paper or a mirror in which case one s knowledge of the digestive process become quite irrelevant
it be doubtless an ingenious idea to call the camel the ship of the desert but it would hardly lead one far in training that useful beast
o aristotle

it be doubtless almost incredible to instruct mind of the present day that a boy of twelve not belong strictly to the mass who be now understand to have the monopoly of mental darkness should have have no distinct idea how there come to be such a thing as latin on this earth yet so it be with tom

but it be worth purchasing even at the heavy price of the latin grammar the happiness of see the bright light in the parlor at home as the gig pass noiselessly over the snow-cover bridge the happiness of pass from the cold air to the warmth and the kiss and the smile of that familiar hearth where the pattern of the rug and the grate and the fire-iron be first idea that it be no more possible to criticise than the solidity and extension of matter
there be no sense of ease like the ease we feel in those scene where we be bear where object become dear to we before we have know the labor of choice and where the outer world seem only an extension of our own personality we accept and love it as we accept our own sense of existence and our own limb
very commonplace even ugly that furniture of our early home might look if it be put up to auction an improved taste in upholstery scorn it and be not the strive after something well and well in our surrounding the grand characteristic that distinguish man from the brute or to satisfy a scrupulous accuracy of definition that distinguish the british man from the foreign brute
but heaven know where that strive might lead we if our affection have not a trick of twining round those old inferior thing if the love and sanctity of our life have no deep immovable root in memory
one s delight in an elderberry bush overhang the confused leafage of a hedgerow bank as a more gladden sight than the fine cistus or fuchsia spread itself on the soft undulating turf be an entirely unjustifiable preference to a nursery-gardener or to any of those regulate mind who be free from the weakness of any attachment that do not rest on a demonstrable superiority of quality
and there be no well reason for prefer this elderberry bush than that it stir an early memory that it be no novelty in my life speak to i merely through my present sensibility to form and color but the long companion of my existence that weave itself into my joy when joy be vivid
chapter ii the christmas holidays fine old christmas with the snowy hair and ruddy face have do his duty that year in the noblest fashion and have set off his rich gift of warmth and color with all the heighten contrast of frost and snow
snow lie on the croft and river-bank in undulation soft than the limb of infancy it lie with the neatli finished border on every slop roof make the dark-red gable stand out with a new depth of color it weigh heavily on the laurel and fir-tree till it fall from they with a shudder sound it clothe the rough turnip-field with whiteness and make the sheep look like dark blotch the gate be all block up with the slop drift and here and there a disregarded four-footed beast stand as if petrify in unrecumbent sadness there be no gleam no shadow for the heaven too be one still pale cloud no sound or motion in anything but the dark river that flow and moan like an unreste sorrow
but old christmas smile as he lay this cruel-seem spell on the outdoor world for he mean to light up home with new brightness to deepen all the richness of indoor color and give a keener edge of delight to the warm fragrance of food he mean to prepare a sweet imprisonment that would strengthen the primitive fellowship of kindred and make the sunshine of familiar human face as welcome as the hide day-star
his kindness fall but hardly on the homeless fell but hardly on the home where the hearth be not very warm and where the food have little fragrance where the human face have have no sunshine in they but rather the leaden blank-eyed gaze of unexpectant want
but the fine old season mean well and if he have not learn the secret how to bless man impartially it be because his father time with ever-unrelenting unrelenting purpose still hide that secret in his own mighty slow-beat heart
and yet this christmas day in spite of tom s fresh delight in home be not he think somehow or other quite so happy as it have always be before
the red berry be just as abundant on the holly and he and maggie have dress all the window and mantlepiece and picture-frame on christmas eve with as much taste as ever wedding the thick-set scarlet cluster with branch of the black-berry ivy
there have be sing under the window after midnight supernatural singing maggie always feel in spite of tom s contemptuous insistence that the singer be old patch the parish clerk and the rest of the church choir she tremble with awe when their carolling break in upon her dream and the image of man in fustian clothe be always thrust away by the vision of angel rest on the part cloud
the midnight chant have help as usual to lift the morning above the level of common day and then there be the smell of hot toast and ale from the kitchen at the breakfast hour the favorite anthem the green bough and the short sermon give the appropriate festal character to the church-going and aunt and uncle moss with all their seven child be look like so many reflector of the bright parlor-fire when the church-goer come back stamp the snow from their foot
the plum-pudding be of the same handsome roundness as ever and come in with the symbolic blue flame around it as if it have be heroically snatch from the nether fire into which it have be throw by dyspeptic puritans the dessert be as splendid as ever with its golden orange brown nut and the crystalline light and dark of apple-jelly and damson cheese in all these thing christmas be as it have always be since tom could remember it be only distinguish it by anything by superior sliding and snowball

chapter iii the new schoolfellow it be a cold wet january day on which tom go back to school a day quite in keep with this severe phase of his destiny

all this you remember happen in those dark age when there be no school of design before schoolmaster be invariably man of scrupulous integrity and before the clergy be all man of enlarged mind and varied culture
in those less favored day it be no fable that there be other clergyman besides mr
stelling who have narrow intellect and large want and whose income by a logical confusion to which fortune be a female as well as blindfold be peculiarly liable be proportion not to their want but to their intellect with which income have clearly no inherent relation
the problem these gentleman have to solve be to readjust the proportion between their want and their income and since want be not easily starve to death the simple method appear to be to raise their income
there be but one way of do this any of those low calling in which man be oblige to do good work at a low price be forbid to clergyman be it their fault if their only resource be to turn out very poor work at a high price

a method of education sanction by the long practice of our venerable ancestor be not to give way before the exceptional dulness of a boy who be merely live at the time then present

book iii the downfall chapter i what have happen at home when mr
tulliver first know the fact that the law-suit be decide against he and that pivart and wakem be triumphant every one who happen to observe he at the time think that for so confident and hot-temper a man he bear the blow remarkably well
he think so himself he think he be go to show that if wakem or anybody else consider he crush they would find themselves mistaken
he could not refuse to see that the cost of this protract suit would take more than he possess to pay they but he appear to himself to be full of expedient by which he could ward off any result but such as be tolerable and could avoid the appearance of break down in the world
all the obstinacy and defiance of his nature drive out of their old channel find a vent for themselves in the immediate formation of plan by which he would meet his difficulty and remain mr
tulliver of dorlcote mill in spite of they

there be furley who hold the mortgage on the land a reasonable fellow who would see his own interest mr

who would neglect such a profitable investment
certainly not furley for mr

there be no doubt in the miller s mind that furley would do just what be desirable and if he do--why thing would not be so very much bad
mr
tulliver and his family must live more meagrely and humbly but it would only be till the profit of the business have pay off furley s advance and that might be while mr

tulliver s banking book less pleasant reading than a man might desire toward christmas
well
he have never be one of those poor-spirited sneak who would refuse to give a help hand to a fellow-traveller in this puzzle world
the really vexatious business be the fact that some month ago the creditor who have lend he the five hundred pound to repay mrs
glegg have become uneasy about his money set on by wakem of course and mr
tulliver still confident that he should gain his suit and find it eminently inconvenient to raise the say sum until that desirable issue have take place have rashly accede to the demand that he should give a bill of sale on his household furniture and some other effect as security in lieu of the bond
it be all one he have say to himself he should soon pay off the money and there be no harm in give that security any more than another
but now the consequence of this bill of sale occur to he in a new light and he remember that the time be close at hand when it would be enforce unless the money be repay
two month ago he would have declare stoutly that he would never be beholden to his wife s friend but now he tell himself as stoutly that it be nothing but right and natural that bessy should go to the pullets and explain the thing to they they would hardly let bessy s furniture be sell and it might be security to pullet if he advance the money there would after all be no gift or favor in the matter
mr
tulliver would never have ask for anything from so poor-spirited a fellow for himself but bessy might do so if she like
it be precisely the proud and most obstinate man who be the most liable to shift their position and contradict themselves in this sudden manner everything be easy to they than to face the simple fact that they have be thoroughly defeat and must begin life anew
and mr
tulliver you perceive though nothing more than a superior miller and maltster be as proud and obstinate as if he have be a very lofty personage in whom such disposition might be a source of that conspicuous far-echo tragedy which sweep the stage in regal robe and make the dull chronicl sublime
the pride and obstinacy of miller and other insignificant people whom you pass unnoticingly on the road every day have their tragedy too but it be of that unwept hidden sort that go on from generation to generation and leave no record such tragedy perhaps as lie in the conflict of young soul hungry for joy under a lot make suddenly hard to they under the dreariness of a home where the morning bring no promise with it and where the unexpectant discontent of worn and disappointed parent weigh on the child like a damp thick air in which all the function of life be depressed or such tragedy as lie in the slow or sudden death that follow on a bruise passion though it may be a death that find only a parish funeral
there be certain animal to which tenacity of position be a law of life they can never flourish again after a single wrench and there be certain human being to whom predominance be a law of life they can only sustain humiliation so long as they can refuse to believe in it and in their own conception predominate still
mr
tulliver be still predominate in his own imagination as he approach st

to mrs

poor child

i shall write it book iv the valley of humiliation chapter i a variation of protestantism unknown to bossuet journeying down the rhone on a summer s day you have perhaps feel the sunshine make dreary by those ruin village which stud the bank in certain part of its course tell how the swift river once rise like an angry destroy god sweep down the feeble generation whose breath be in their nostril and make their dwelling a desolation
strange contrast you may have think between the effect produce on we by these dismal remnant of commonplace house which in their good day be but the sign of a sordid life belong in all its detail to our own vulgar era and the effect produce by those ruin on the castle rhine which have crumble and mellow into such harmony with the green and rocky steep that they seem to have a natural fitness like the mountain-pine nay even in the day when they be build they must have have this fitness as if they have be raise by an earth-bear race who have inherit from their mighty parent a sublime instinct of form
and that be a day of romance if those robber-baron be somewhat grim and drunken ogre they have a certain grandeur of the wild beast in them they be forest boar with tusk tear and rending not the ordinary domestic grunter they represent the demon force forever in collision with beauty virtue and the gentle us of life they make a fine contrast in the picture with the wandering minstrel the soft-lipped princess the pious recluse and the timid israelite
that be a time of color when the sunlight fall on glance steel and float banner a time of adventure and fierce struggle nay of living religious art and religious enthusiasm for be not cathedral build in those day and do not great emperor leave their western palace to die before the infidel stronghold in the sacred east
therefore it be that these rhine castle thrill i with a sense of poetry they belong to the grand historic life of humanity and raise up for i the vision of an echo
but these dead-tint hollow-eye angular skeleton of village on the rhone oppress i with the feeling that human life--very much of it--be a narrow ugly grovel existence which even calamity do not elevate but rather tend to exhibit in all its bare vulgarity of conception and i have a cruel conviction that the life these ruin be the trace of be part of a gross sum of obscure vitality that will be sweep into the same oblivion with the generation of ant and beaver
perhaps something akin to this oppressive feeling may have weigh upon you in watch this old-fashioned family life on the bank of the floss which even sorrow hardly suffice to lift above the level of the tragi-comic
it be a sordid life you say this of the tullivers and dodson irradiate by no sublime principle no romantic vision no active self-renounce faith move by none of those wild uncontrollable passion which create the dark shadow of misery and crime without that primitive rough simplicity of want that hard submissive ill-pay toil that childlike spelling-out of what nature have write which give its poetry to peasant life
here one have conventional worldly notion and habit without instruction and without polish surely the most prosaic form of human life proud respectability in a gig of unfashionable build worldliness without side-dish
observe these people narrowly even when the iron hand of misfortune have shake they from their unquestione hold on the world one see little trace of religion still less of a distinctively christian creed
their belief in the unseen so far as it manifest itself at all seem to be rather a pagan kind their moral notion though hold with strong tenacity seem to have no standard beyond hereditary custom
you could not live among such people you be stifle for want of an outlet toward something beautiful great or noble you be irritated with these dull man and woman as a kind of population out of keep with the earth on which they live with this rich plain where the great river flow forever onward and link the small pulse of the old english town with the beating of the world s mighty heart
a vigorous superstition that lash its god or lash its own back seem to be more congruous with the mystery of the human lot than the mental condition of these emmet-like dodsons and tullivers
i share with you this sense of oppressive narrowness but it be necessary that we should feel it if we care to understand how it act on the life of tom and maggie how it have act on young nature in many generation that in the onward tendency of human thing have rise above the mental level of the generation before they to which they have be nevertheless tie by the strong fibre of their heart
the suffering whether of martyr or victim which belong to every historical advance of mankind be represent in this way in every town and by hundred of obscure hearth and we need not shrink from this comparison of small thing with great for do not science tell we that its high strive be after the ascertainment of a unity which shall bind the small thing with the great
in natural science i have understand there be nothing petty to the mind that have a large vision of relation and to which every single object suggest a vast sum of condition
it be surely the same with the observation of human life
certainly the religious and moral idea of the dodsons and tullivers be of too specific a kind to be arrive at deductively from the statement that they be part of the protestant population of great britain
their theory of life have its core of soundness as all theory must have on which decent and prosperous family have be rear and have flourish but it have the very slight tincture of theology
if in the maiden day of the dodson sister their bible open more easily at some part than other it be because of dried tulip-petal which have be distribute quite impartially without preference for the historical devotional or doctrinal
their religion be of a simple semi-pagan kind but there be no heresy in it if heresy properly mean choice for they donot know there be any other religion except that of chapel-goer which appear to run in family like asthma
how should they know
the vicar of their pleasant rural parish be not a controversialist but a good hand at whist and one who have a joke always ready for a bloom female parishioner
the religion of the dodson consist in revering whatever be customary and respectable it be necessary to be baptize else one could not be bury in the church-yard and to take the sacrament before death as a security against more dimly understand peril but it be of equal necessity to have the proper pall-bearer and well-cure ham at one s funeral and to leave an unimpeachable will
a dodson would not be tax with the omission of anything that be become or that belong to that eternal fitness of thing which be plainly indicate in the practice of the most substantial parishioner and in the family traditions such as obedience to parent faithfulness to kindre industry rigid honesty thrift the thorough scouring of wooden and copper utensil the hoarding of coin likely to disappear from the currency the production of first-rate commodity for the market and the general preference of whatever be home-make
the dodsons be a very proud race and their pride lie in the utter frustration of all desire to tax they with a breach of traditional duty or propriety
a wholesome pride in many respect since it identify honor with perfect integrity thoroughness of work and faithfulness to admit rule and society owe some worthy quality in many of her member to mother of the dodson class who make their butter and their fromenty well and would have feel disgrace to make it otherwise
to be honest and poor be never a dodson motto still less to seem rich though be poor rather the family badge be to be honest and rich and not only rich but rich than be suppose
to live respect and have the proper bearer at your funeral be an achievement of the end of existence that would be entirely nullify if on the reading of your will you sink in the opinion of your fellow-man either by turn out to be poor than they expect or by leave your money in a capricious manner without strict regard to degree of kin
the right thing must always be do toward kindre
the right thing be to correct they severely if they be other than a credit to the family but still not to alienate from they the small rightful share in the family shoebuckle and other property
a conspicuous quality in the dodson character be its genuineness its vice and virtue alike be phase of a proud honest egoism which have a hearty dislike to whatever make against its own credit and interest and would be frankly hard of speech to inconvenient kin but would never forsake or ignore them would not let they want bread but only require they to eat it with bitter herb
the same sort of traditional belief run in the tulliver vein but it be carry in rich blood have element of generous imprudence warm affection and hot-temper rashness

tulliver s grandfather have be hear to say that he be descend from one ralph tulliver a wonderfully clever fellow who have ruin himself

on the other hand nobody have ever hear of a dodson who have ruin himself it be not the way of that family

chapter ii the torn nest be pierce by the thorns there be something sustain in the very agitation that accompany the first shock of trouble just as an acute pain be often a stimulus and produce an excitement which be transient strength
it be in the slow change life that follow in the time when sorrow have become stale and have no long an emotive intensity that counteract its pain in the time when day follow day in dull unexpectant sameness and trial be a dreary routine it be then that despair threaten it be then that the peremptory hunger of the soul be feel and eye and ear be strain after some unlearned secret of our existence which shall give to endurance the nature of satisfaction
this time of utmost need be come to maggie with her short span of thirteen year

but the constant presence of her mother s regretful bewilderment be less painful to maggie than that of her father s sullen incommunicative depression

it be something cruelly incomprehensible to youthful nature this sombre sameness in middle-aged and elderly people whose life have result in disappointment and discontent to whose face a smile become so strange that the sad line all about the lip and brow seem to take no notice of it and it hurry away again for want of a welcome

tulliver linger nowhere away from home he hurry away from market he refuse all invitation to stay and chat as in old time in the house where he call on business

the little store of sovereign in the tin box seem to be the only sight that bring a faint beam of pleasure into the miller s eyes faint and transient for it be soon dispel by the thought that the time would be long--perhaps long than his life before the narrow saving could remove the hateful incubus of debt

these narrow notion about debt hold by the old fashioned tullivers may perhaps excite a smile on the face of many reader in these day of wide commercial view and wide philosophy accord to which everything right itself without any trouble of our

chapter iii a voice from the past one afternoon when the chestnut be come into flower maggie have bring her chair outside the front door and be seat there with a book on her knee

i cannot give you mumps cause hewould break his heart to go away from i--eh mumps what do you say you riff-raff mumps decline to express himself more diffusely than by a single affirmative movement of his tail but iwould get you a pup miss an welcome no thank you bob
