it might as well ha be narrow maggie thus exalt into bob s exalt madonna laugh in spite of herself at which her worshipper s blue eye twinkle too and under these favor auspex he touch his cap and walk away
the day of chivalry be not go notwithstanding burke s grand dirge over they they live still in that far-off worship pay by many a youth and man to the woman of whom he never dream that he shall touch so much as her little finger or the hem of her robe
bob with the pack on his back have as respectful an adoration for this dark-eye maiden as if he have be a knight in armor call aloud on her name as he prick on to the fight
that gleam of merriment soon die away from maggie s face and perhaps only make the return gloom deep by contrast
she be too dispirit even to like answer question about bob s present of book and she carry they away to her bedroom lay they down there and seat herself on her one stool without care to look at they just yet
she lean her cheek against the window-frame and think that the light-hearted bob have a lot much happy than her
maggie s sense of loneliness and utter privation of joy have deepen with the brightness of advance spring
all the favorite outdoor nook about home which seem to have do their part with her parent in nurture and cherish she be now mix up with the home-sadness and gather no smile from the sunshine
every affection every delight the poor child have have be like an ache nerve to she
there be no music for her any more no piano no harmonized voice no delicious string instrument with their passionate cry of imprison spirit send a strange vibration through her frame
and of all her school-life there be nothing leave she now but her little collection of school-book which she turn over with a sickening sense that she know they all and they be all barren of comfort
even at school she have often wish for book with more in they everything she learn there seem like the end of long thread that snap immediately
and now--without the indirect charm of school-emulation--telemaque be mere bran so be the hard dry question on christian doctrine there be no flavor in they no strength
sometimes maggie think she could have be content with absorb fancy if she could have have all scott s novel and all byron s poems then perhaps she might have find happiness enough to dull her sensibility to her actual daily life
and yet they be hardly what she want
she could make dream-world of her own but no dream-world would satisfy she now
she want some explanation of this hard real life the unhappy-look father seat at the dull breakfast-table the childish bewildered mother the little sordid task that fill the hour or the more oppressive emptiness of weary joyless leisure the need of some tender demonstrative love the cruel sense that tom donot mind what she think or feel and that they be no long playfellow together the privation of all pleasant thing that have come to her more than to others she want some key that would enable she to understand and in understanding to endure the heavy weight that have fall on her young heart
if she have be teach real learning and wisdom such as great man know she think she should have hold the secret of life if she have only book that she might learn for herself what wise man know
saint and martyr have never interest maggie so much as sage and poet
she know little of saint and martyr and have gather as a general result of her teaching that they be a temporary provision against the spread of catholicism and have all die at smithfield
in one of these meditation it occur to she that she have forget tom s school-book which have be send home in his trunk
but she find the stock unaccountably shrink down to the few old one which have be well thumbed the latin dictionary and grammar a delectus a tear eutropius the well-wear virgil aldrich s logic and the exasperating euclid
still latin euclid and logic would surely be a considerable step in masculine wisdom in that knowledge which make man content and even glad to live
not that the yearning for effectual wisdom be quite unmixed a certain mirage would now and then rise on the desert of the future in which she seem to see herself honor for her surprising attainment
and so the poor child with her soul s hunger and her illusion of self-flattery begin to nibble at this thick-rinded fruit of the tree of knowledge fill her vacant hour with latin geometry and the form of the syllogism and feel a gleam of triumph now and then that her understanding be quite equal to these peculiarly masculine study
for a week or two she go on resolutely enough though with an occasional sinking of heart as if she have set out toward the promised land alone and find it a thirsty trackless uncertain journey
in the severity of her early resolution she would take aldrich out into the field and then look off her book toward the sky where the lark be twinkle or to the reed and bush by the river from which the waterfowl rustle forth on its anxious awkward flight with a startled sense that the relation between aldrich and this live world be extremely remote for she
the discouragement deepen as the day go on and the eager heart gain fast and fast on the patient mind
somehow when she sit at the window with her book her eye would fix themselves blankly on the outdoor sunshine then they would fill with tear and sometimes if her mother be not in the room the study would all end in sobbing
she rebel against her lot she faint under its loneliness and fit even of anger and hatred toward her father and mother who be so unlike what she would have they to be toward tom who check she and meet her thought or feel always by some thwarting difference would flow out over her affection and conscience like a lava stream and frighten she with a sense that it be not difficult for she to become a demon
then her brain would be busy with wild romance of a flight from home in search of something less sordid and dreary she would go to some great man--walter scott perhaps--and tell he how wretched and how clever she be and he would surely do something for she
but in the middle of her vision her father would perhaps enter the room for the evening and surprise that she sit still without notice he would say complainingly come be i to fetch my slipper myself the voice pierce through maggie like a sword there be another sadness besides her own and she have be think of turn she back on it and forsake it
this afternoon the sight of bob s cheerful freckle face have give her discontent a new direction
she think it be part of the hardship of her life that there be lay upon she the burthen of large want than other seem to feel that she have to endure this wide hopeless yearning for that something whatever it be that be great and well on this earth
she wish she could have be like bob with his easily satisfied ignorance or like tom who have something to do on which he could fix his mind with a steady purpose and disregard everything else
poor child
as she lean her head against the window-frame with her hand clasp tighter and tight and her foot beat the ground she be as lonely in her trouble as if she have be the only gril in the civilized world of that day who have come out of her school-life with a soul untraine for inevitable struggle with no other part of her inherit share in the hard-win treasure of thought which generation of painful toil have lay up for the race of man than shred and patch of feeble literature and false history with much futile information about saxon and other king of doubtful example but unhappily quite without that knowledge of the irreversible law within and without she which govern the habit become morality and develop the feeling of submission and dependence become religion as lonely in her trouble as if every other girl besides herself have be cherish and watch over by elder mind not forgetful of their own early time when need be keen and impulse strong
at last maggie s eye glance down on the book that lie on the window-shelf and she half forsook her reverie to turn over listlessly the leaf of the portrait gallery but she soon push this aside to examine the little row of book tie together with string
beauties of the spectator rasselas economy of human life gregory s letters she know the sort of matter that be inside all these the christian year that seem to be a hymnbook and she lay it down again but thomas a kempis the name have come across she in her reading and she feel the satisfaction which every one know of get some idea to attach to a name that stray solitary in the memory
she take up the little old clumsy book with some curiosity it have the corner turn down in many place and some hand now forever quiet have make at certain passage strong pen-and-ink mark long since brown by time
maggie turn from leaf to leaf and read where the quiet hand point know that the love of thyself doth hurt thee more than anything in the world
if thou seek this or that and wouldst be here or there to enjoy thy own will and pleasure thou shalt never be quiet nor free from care for in everything somewhat will be want and in every place there will be some that will cross thee
both above and below which way soever thou dost turn thee everywhere thou shalt find the cross and everywhere of necessity thou must have patience if thou wilt have inward peace and enjoy an everlasting crown
if thou desir to mount unto this height thou must set out courageously and lie the axe to the root that thou mayest pluck up and destroy that hide inordinate inclination to thyself and unto all private and earthly good
on this sin that a man inordinately loveth himself almost all dependeth whatsoever be thoroughly to be overcome which evil being once overcome and subdue there will presently ensue great peace and tranquillity
it be but little thou sufferest in comparison of they that have suffer so much be so strongly tempt so grievously afflict so many way try and exercise
thou ought therefore to call to mind the more heavy suffering of other that thou mayest the easy bear thy little adversity
and if they seem not little unto thee beware lest thy impatience be the cause thereof
bless be those ear that receive the whisper of the divine voice and listen not to the whispering of the world
blessed be those ear which hearken not unto the voice which soundeth outwardly but unto the truth which teacheth inwardly a strange thrill of awe pass through maggie while she read as if she have be waken in the night by a strain of solemn music tell of being whose soul have be astir while hers be in stupor
she go on from one brown mark to another where the quiet hand seem to point hardly conscious that she be read seem rather to listen while a low voice say why dost thou here gaze about since this be not the place of thy rest
in heaven ought to be thy dwelling and all earthly thing be to be look on as they forward thy journey thither
all thing pass away and thou together with they
beware thou cleavest not unto they lest thou be entangle and perish
if a man should give all his substance yet it be as nothing
and if he should do great penance yet be they but little
and if he should attain to all knowledge he be yet far off
and if he should be of great virtue and very fervent devotion yet be there much wanting to wit one thing which be most necessary for he
what be that
that have leave all he leave himself and go wholly out of himself and retain nothing of self-love
i have often say unto thee and now again i say the same forsake thyself resign thyself and thou shalt enjoy much inward peace
then shall all vain imagination evil perturbation and superfluous care fly away then shall immoderate fear leave thee and inordinate love shall die maggie draw a long breath and push her heavy hair back as if to see a sudden vision more clearly
here then be a secret of life that would enable she to renounce all other secret here be a sublime height to be reach without the help of outward thing here be insight and strength and conquest to be win by mean entirely within her own soul where a supreme teacher be wait to be hear
it flash through she like the suddenly apprehend solution of a problem that all the misery of her young life have come from fix her heart on her own pleasure as if that be the central necessity of the universe and for the first time she see the possibility of shift the position from which she look at the gratification of her own desires of take she stand out of herself and look at her own life as an insignificant part of a divinely guide whole
she read on and on in the old book devour eagerly the dialogue with the invisible teacher the pattern of sorrow the source of all strength return to it after she have be call away and read till the sun go down behind the willow
with all the hurry of an imagination that could never rest in the present she sit in the deepening twilight form plan of self-humiliation and entire devotedness and in the ardor of first discovery renunciation seem to she the entrance into that satisfaction which she have so long be craving in vain
she have not perceive--how could she until she have live longer the inmost truth of the old monk s out-pouring that renunciation remain sorrow though a sorrow bear willingly
maggie be still pant for happiness and be in ecstasy because she have find the key to it
she know nothing of doctrine and system of mysticism or quietism but this voice out of the far-off middle age be the direct communication of a human soul s belief and experience and come to maggie as an unquestioned message
i suppose that be the reason why the small old-fashioned book for which you need only pay sixpence at a book-stall work miracle to this day turn bitter water into sweetness while expensive sermon and treatise newly issue leave all thing as they be before
it be write down by a hand that wait for the heart s prompt it be the chronicle of a solitary hidden anguish struggle trust and triumph not write on velvet cushion to teach endurance to those who be treading with bleed foot on the stone
and so it remain to all time a lasting record of human need and human consolation the voice of a brother who age ago feel and suffer and renounced in the cloister perhaps with serge gown and tonsure head with much chant and long fast and with a fashion of speech different from ours but under the same silent far-off heaven and with the same passionate desire the same striving the same failure the same weariness
in write the history of unfashionable family one be apt to fall into a tone of emphasis which be very far from be the tone of good society where principle and belief be not only of an extremely moderate kind but be always presuppose no subject be eligible but such as can be touch with a light and graceful irony
but then good society have its claret and its velvet carpet its dinner-engagement six week deep its opera and its faery ball-room rid off its ennui on thoroughbred horse lounge at the club have to keep clear of crinoline vortex get its science do by faraday and its religion by the superior clergy who be to be meet in the good houses how should it have time or need for belief and emphasis
but good society float on gossamer wing of light irony be of very expensive production require nothing less than a wide and arduous national life condense in unfragrant deafen factory cramp itself in mine sweat at furnace grind hammer weave under more or less oppression of carbonic acid or else spread over sheepwalk and scatter in lonely house and hut on the clayey or chalky corn-land where the rainy day look dreary
this wide national life be base entirely on emphasis the emphasis of want which urge it into all the activity necessary for the maintenance of good society and light irony it spend its heavy year often in a chill uncarpeted fashion amidst family discord unsoftene by long corridor
under such circumstance there be many among its myriad of soul who have absolutely need an emphatic belief life in this unpleasurable shape demand some solution even to unspeculative minds just as you inquire into the stuffing of your couch when anything gall you there whereas eider-down and perfect french spring excite no question
some have an emphatic belief in alcohol and seek their ekstasis or outside standing-ground in gin but the rest require something that good society call enthusiasm something that will present motif in an entire absence of high prize something that will give patience and feed human love when the limb ache with weariness and human look be hard upon we something clearly that lie outside personal desire that include resignation for ourselves and active love for what be not ourselves
now and then that sort of enthusiasm find a far-echo voice that come from an experience spring out of the deep need and it be by be bring within the long linger vibration of such a voice that maggie with her girl s face and unnoted sorrow find an effort and a hope that help she through year of loneliness make out a faith for herself without the aid of established authority and appoint guide for they be not at hand and her need be press
from what you know of she you will not be surprised that she throw some exaggeration and wilfulness some pride and impetuosity even into her self-renunciation her own life be still a drama for she in which she demand of herself that her part should be play with intensity
and so it come to pass that she often lose the spirit of humility by be excessive in the outward act she often strove after too high a flight and come down with her poor little half-fledge wing dabble in the mud
for example she not only determine to work at plain sewing that she might contribute something toward the fund in the tin box but she go in the first instance in her zeal of self-mortification to ask for it at a linen shop in st
ogg s instead of get it in a more quiet and indirect way and could see nothing but what be entirely wrong and unkind nay persecuting in tom s reproof of she for this unnecessary act
i donot like my sister to do such thing say tom iwill take care that the debt be pay without your lower yourself in that way surely there be some tenderness and bravery mingle with the worldliness and self-assertion of that little speech but maggie hold it as dross overlook the grain of gold and take tom s rebuke as one of she outward cross
tom be very hard to she she use to think in she long night-watchings to she who have always love he so and then she strove to be content with that hardness and to require nothing
that be the path we all like when we set out on our abandonment of egoism the path of martyrdom and endurance where the palm-branch grow rather than the steep highway of tolerance just allowance and self-blame where there be no leafy honor to be gather and wear
the old book virgil euclid and aldrich--that wrinkle fruit of the tree of knowledge--have be all lay by for maggie have turn she back on the vain ambition to share the thought of the wise
in her first ardor she fling away the book with a sort of triumph that she have rise above the need of they and if they have be her own she would have burn they believe that she would never repent
she read so eagerly and constantly in her three book the bible thomas a kempis and the christian year no long reject as a hymn-book that they fill her mind with a continual stream of rhythmic memory and she be too ardently learn to see all nature and life in the light of her new faith to need any other material for her mind to work on as she sit with her well-ply needle make shirt and other complicated stitching falsely call plain by no mean plain to maggie since wristband and sleeve and the like have a capability of be sew in wrong side outward in moment of mental wandering
hang diligently over her sewing maggie be a sight any one might have be pleased to look at
that new inward life of her notwithstanding some volcanic upheaving of imprison passion yet shine out in her face with a tender soft light that mingle itself as add loveliness with the gradually enrich color and outline of her blossoming youth
her mother feel the change in she with a sort of puzzled wonder that maggie should be grow up so good it be amazing that this once contrairy child be become so submissive so backward to assert her own will
maggie use to look up from her work and find her mother s eye fix upon she they be watch and wait for the large young glance as if her eld frame get some needful warmth from it
the mother be get fond of her tall brown girl the only bit of furniture now on which she could bestow her anxiety and pride and maggie in spite of her own ascetic wish to have no personal adornment be oblige to give way to her mother about her hair and submit to have the abundant black lock plait into a coronet on the summit of her head after the pitiable fashion of those antiquated time

tulliver iwould trouble enough with your hair once so maggie glad of anything that would soothe her mother and cheer their long day together consent to the vain decoration and show a queenly head above her old frock steadily refuse however to look at herself in the glass
mrs

i know well enough what shewould be before now it s nothing new to i

he sit patiently enough while she read he a chapter or say something timidly when they be alone together about trouble be turn into a blessing
he take it all as part of his daughter s goodness which make his misfortune the sadder to he because they damage her chance in life
in a mind charge with an eager purpose and an unsatisfied vindictiveness there be no room for new feeling mr
tulliver do not want spiritual consolation--he want to shake off the degradation of debt and to have his revenge
book v wheat and tares chapter i in the red deeps the family sitting-room be a long room with a window at each end one look toward the croft and along the ripple to the bank of the floss the other into the mill-yard
maggie be sit with her work against the latter window when she see mr
wakem enter the yard as usual on his fine black horse but not alone as usual
some one be with him a figure in a cloak on a handsome pony
maggie have hardly time to feel that it be philip come back before they be in front of the window and he be raise his hat to she while his father catch the movement by a side-glance look sharply round at they both
maggie hurry away from the window and carry her work upstairs for mr
wakem sometimes come in and inspect the book and maggie feel that the meeting with philip would be rob of all pleasure in the presence of the two father
some day perhaps she could see he when they could just shake hand and she could tell he that she remember his goodness to tom and the thing he have say to she in the old day though they could never be friend any more
it be not at all agitate to maggie to see philip again she retain she childish gratitude and pity toward he and remember his cleverness and in the early week of her loneliness she have continually recall the image of he among the people who have be kind to she in life often wish she have he for a brother and a teacher as they have fancy it might have be in their talk together
but that sort of wish have be banish along with other dream that savor of seek her own will and she think besides that philip might be alter by his life abroad he might have become worldly and really not care about she say anything to he now
and yet his face be wonderfully little altered it be only a large more manly copy of the pale small-feature boy s face with the gray eye and the boyish wave brown hair there be the old deformity to awaken the old pity and after all her meditation maggie feel that she really should like to say a few word to he
he might still be melancholy as he always use to be and like she to look at he kindly
she wonder if he remember how he use to like her eye with that think maggie glance toward the square looking-glass which be condemn to hang with its face toward the wall and she half start from her seat to reach it down but she check herself and snatch up her work try to repress the rise wish by force her memory to recall snatch of hymn until she see philip and his father return along the road and she could go down again
it be far on in june now and maggie be incline to lengthen the daily walk which be her one indulgence but this day and the following she be so busy with work which must be finish that she never go beyond the gate and satisfy her need of the open air by sit out of door
one of her frequent walk when she be not oblige to go to st
ogg s be to a spot that lie beyond what be call the hill an insignificant rise of ground crown by tree lie along the side of the road which run by the gate of dorlcote mill
insignificant i call it because in height it be hardly more than a bank but there may come moment when nature make a mere bank a means toward a fateful result and that be why i ask you to imagine this high bank crown with tree make an uneven wall for some quarter of a mile along the left side of dorlcote mill and the pleasant field behind it bound by the murmur ripple
just where this line of bank slop down again to the level a by-road turn off and lead to the other side of the rise where it be break into very capricious hollow and mound by the working of an exhausted stone-quarry so long exhaust that both mound and hollow be now clothe with bramble and tree and here and there by a stretch of grass which a few sheep keep close-nibble
in her childish day maggie hold this place call the red deeps in very great awe and need all her confidence in tom s bravery to reconcile she to an excursion thither visions of robber and fierce animal haunt every hollow
but now it have the charm for she which any break ground any mimic rock and ravine have for the eye that rest habitually on the level especially in summer when she could sit on a grassy hollow under the shadow of a branch ash stoop aslant from the steep above she and listen to the hum of insect like tiny bell on the garment of silence or see the sunlight pierce the distant bough as if to chase and drive home the truant heavenly blue of the wild hyacinth
in this june time too the dog-rose be in their glory and that be an additional reason why maggie should direct her walk to the red deeps rather than to any other spot on the first day she be free to wander at her will a pleasure she love so well that sometimes in her ardor of renunciation she think she ought to deny herself the frequent indulgence in it
you may see she now as she walk down the favorite turning and enter the deep by a narrow path through a group of scotch fir her tall figure and old lavender gown visible through an hereditary black silk shawl of some wide-mesh net-like material and now she be sure of be unseen she take off her bonnet and tie it over her arm
one would certainly suppose she to be far on in life than she seventeenth year--perhaps because of the slow resign sadness of the glance from which all search and unrest seem to have depart perhaps because her broad-cheste figure have the mould of early womanhood
youth and health have withstand well the involuntary and voluntary hardship of her lot and the night in which she have lie on the hard floor for a penance have leave no obvious trace the eye be liquid the brown cheek be firm and round the full lip be red
with her dark coloring and jet crown surmount her tall figure she seem to have a sort of kinship with the grand scotch fir at which she be look up as if she love they well
yet one have a sense of uneasiness in look at her a sense of oppose element of which a fierce collision be imminent surely there be a hushed expression such as one often see in old face under borderless cap out of keep with the resistant youth which one expect to flash out in a sudden passionate glance that will dissipate all the quietude like a damp fire leap out again when all seem safe
but maggie herself be not uneasy at this moment
she be clamly enjoy the free air while she look up at the old fir-tree and think that those broken end of branch be the record of past storm which have only make the red stem soar higher
but while her eye be still turn upward she become conscious of a move shadow cast by the evening sun on the grassy path before she and look down with a startled gesture to see philip wakem who first raise his hat and then blush deeply come forward to she and put out his hand
maggie too color with surprise which soon give way to pleasure
she put out her hand and look down at the deformed figure before she with frank eye fill for the moment with nothing but the memory of her child s feelings a memory that be always strong in she
she be the first to speak
you startle i she say smile faintly i never meet any one here
how come you to be walk here
do you come to meet i it be impossible not to perceive that maggie feel herself a child again
yes i do say philip still embarrassed i wish to see you very much
i watch a long while yesterday on the bank near your house to see if you would come out but you never come
then i watch again to-day and when i see the way you take i keep you in sight and come down the bank behind there
i hope you will not be displease with i no say maggie with simple seriousness walk on as if she mean philip to accompany she ibe very glad you come for i wish very much to have an opportunity of speak to you
ihave never forget how good you be long ago to tom and i too but i be not sure that you would remember we so well
tom and i have have a great deal of trouble since then and i think that make one think more of what happen before the trouble come i cannot believe that you have think of i so much as i have think of you say philip timidly
do you know when i be away i make a picture of you as you look that morning in the study when you say you would not forget i philip draw a large miniature-case from his pocket and open it
maggie see her old self lean on a table with her black lock hang down behind her ear look into space with strange dreamy eye
it be a water-color sketch of real merit as a portrait
oh dear say maggie smiling and flush with pleasure what a queer little girl i be
i remember myself with my hair in that way in that pink frock
i really be like a gypsy
i dare say i be now she add after a little pause be i like what you expect i to be the word might have be those of a coquette but the full bright glance maggie turn on philip be not that of a coquette
she really do hope he like her face as it be now but it be simply the rise again of her innate delight in admiration and love

her eyelid fall lower but she do not turn away her head and philip continue to look at she

he do not see that it would have be well to soothe the interval with a new hope and prevent the delirium of a too sudden elation
at the time of maggie s first meeting with philip tom have already nearly a hundred and fifty pound of his own capital and while they be walk by the evening light in the red deeps he by the same evening light be rid into laceham proud of be on his first journey on behalf of guest co and revolve in his mind all the chance that by the end of another year he should have double his gain lift off the obloquy of debt from his father s name and perhaps--for he should be twenty-one--have get a new start for himself on a high platform of employment
do he not desire it
he be quite sure that he do
chapter iii the wavering balance i say that maggie go home that evening from the red deeps with a mental conflict already begin
you have see clearly enough in her interview with philip what that conflict be
here suddenly be an opening in the rocky wall which shut in the narrow valley of humiliation where all her prospect be the remote unfathomed sky and some of the memory-haunt earthly delight be no long out of her reach
she might have book converse affection she might hear tiding of the world from which her mind have not yet lose its sense of exile and it would be a kindness to philip too who be pitiable clearly not happy
and perhaps here be an opportunity indicate for make her mind more worthy of its high service perhaps the noble complete devoutness could hardly exist without some width of knowledge must she always live in this resign imprisonment
it be so blameless so good a thing that there should be friendship between she and philip the motif that forbid it be so unreasonable so unchristian
but the severe monotonous warning come again and again that she be lose the simplicity and clearness of her life by admit a ground of concealment and that by forsake the simple rule of renunciation she be throw herself under the seductive guidance of illimitable want
she think she have win strength to obey the warning before she allow herself the next week to turn her step in the evening to the red deeps
but while she be resolve to say an affectionate farewell to philip how she look forward to that evening walk in the still fleckered shade of the hollow away from all that be harsh and unlovely to the affectionate admire look that would meet she to the sense of comradeship that childish memory would give to wiser old talk to the certainty that philip would care to hear everything she say which no one else care for
it be a half-hour that it would be very hard to turn she back upon with the sense that there would be no other like it
yet she say what she mean to say she look firm as well as sad
philip i have make up my mind it be right that we should give each other up in everything but memory
i could not see you without concealment--stay i know what you be go to say it be other people s wrong feeling that make concealment necessary but concealment be bad however it may be cause
i feel that it would be bad for i for we both
and then if our secret be discover there would be nothing but misery dreadful anger and then we must part after all and it would be hard when we be use to see each other philip s face have flush and there be a momentary eagerness of expression as if he have be about to resist this decision with all his might
but he control himself and say with assumed calmness well maggie if we must part let we try and forget it for one half hour let we talk together a little while for the last time he take her hand and maggie feel no reason to withdraw it his quietness make she all the more sure she have give he great pain and she want to show he how unwillingly she have give it
they walk together hand in hand in silence

it be an oil-painting
you will look like a tall hamadryad dark and strong and noble just issue from one of the fir-tree when the stem be cast their afternoon shadow on the grass you seem to think more of painting than of anything now philip perhaps i do say philip rather sadly but i think of too many things sow all sort of seed and get no great harvest from any one of they
ibe curse with susceptibility in every direction and effective faculty in none
i care for painting and music i care for classic literature and mediaeval literature and modern literature i flutter all way and fly in none but surely that be a happiness to have so many tastes to enjoy so many beautiful thing when they be within your reach say maggie musingly
it always seem to i a sort of clever stupidity only to have one sort of talent almost like a carrier-pigeon it might be a happiness to have many taste if i be like other man say philip bitterly
i might get some power and distinction by mere mediocrity as they do at least i should get those middling satisfaction which make man content to do without great one
i might think society at st
ogg s agreeable then
but nothing could make life worth the purchase-money of pain to i but some faculty that would lift i above the dead level of provincial existence
yes there be one thing a passion answer as well as a faculty maggie do not hear the last word she be struggle against the consciousness that philip s word have set her own discontent vibrate again as it use to do
i understand what you mean she say though i know so much less than you do
i use to think i could never bear life if it keep on be the same every day and i must always be do thing of no consequence and never know anything great
but dear philip i think we be only like child that some one who be wise be take care of
be it not right to resign ourselves entirely whatever may be deny we
i have find great peace in that for the last two or three year even joy in subdue my own will yes maggie say philip vehemently and you be shut yourself up in a narrow self-delusive fanaticism which be only a way of escape pain by starve into dulness all the high power of your nature
joy and peace be not resignation resignation be the willing endurance of a pain that be not allay that you donot expect to be allay
stupefaction be not resignation and it be stupefaction to remain in ignorance to shut up all the avenue by which the life of your fellow-man might become known to you
i be not resign i be not sure that life be long enough to learn that lesson
you be not resign you be only try to stupefy yourself maggie s lip tremble she feel there be some truth in what philip say and yet there be a deep consciousness that for any immediate application it have to her conduct it be no well than falsity
her double impression correspond to the double impulse of the speaker
philip seriously believe what he say but he say it with vehemence because it make an argument against the resolution that oppose his wish
but maggie s face make more childlike by the gathering tear touch he with a tenderer less egotistic feeling
he take her hand and say gently donot let we think of such thing in this short half-hour maggie
let we only care about be together
we shall be friend in spite of separation
we shall always think of each other
i shall be glad to live as long as you be alive because i shall think there may always come a time when i can--when you will let i help you in some way what a dear good brother you would have be philip say maggie smile through the haze of tear
i think you would have make as much fuss about i and be as pleased for i to love you as would have satisfy even i
you would have love i well enough to bear with i and forgive i everything
that be what i always long that tom should do
i be never satisfied with a little of anything
that be why it be well for i to do without earthly happiness altogether
i never feel that i have enough music i want more instrument play together i want voice to be full and deep
do you ever sing now philip she add abruptly as if she have forget what go before
yes he say every day almost
but my voice be only middling like everything else in i oh sing i something just one song

it will only haunt i
let we walk philip
i must go home she move away so that he be oblige to rise and follow she
maggie he say in a tone of remonstrance donot persist in this wilful senseless privation
it make i wretched to see you benumb and cramp your nature in this way
you be so full of life when you be a child i think you would be a brilliant woman all wit and bright imagination
and it flash out in your face still until you draw that veil of dull quiescence over it why do you speak so bitterly to i philip say maggie
because i foresee it will not end well you can never carry on this self-torture i shall have strength give i say maggie tremulously
no you will not maggie no one have strength give to do what be unnatural
it be mere cowardice to seek safety in negation
no character become strong in that way
you will be throw into the world some day and then every rational satisfaction of your nature that you deny now will assault you like a savage appetite maggie start and pause look at philip with alarm in her face
philip how dare you shake i in this way
you be a tempter no i be not but love give insight maggie and insight often give foreboding
listen to me let i supply you with book do let i see you sometimes be your brother and teacher as you say at lorton
it be less wrong that you should see i than that you should be commit this long suicide maggie feel unable to speak
she shake her head and walk on in silence till they come to the end of the scotch fir and she put out her hand in sign of parting
do you banish i from this place forever then maggie
surely i may come and walk in it sometimes
if i meet you by chance there be no concealment in that it be the moment when our resolution seem about to become irrevocable--when the fatal iron gate be about to close upon we--that test our strength
then after hour of clear reasoning and firm conviction we snatch at any sophistry that will nullify our long struggle and bring we the defeat that we love well than victory
maggie feel her heart leap at this subterfuge of philip s and there pass over her face that almost imperceptible shock which accompany any relief
he see it and they part in silence
philip s sense of the situation be too complete for he not to be visit with glance fear lest he have be intervene too presumptuously in the action of maggie s conscience perhaps for a selfish end
but no he persuade himself his end be not selfish
he have little hope that maggie would ever return the strong feeling he have for she and it must be well for maggie s future life when these petty family obstacle to her freedom have disappear that the present should not be entirely sacrifice and that she should have some opportunity of culture some interchange with a mind above the vulgar level of those she be now condemn to live with
if we only look far enough off for the consequence of our action we can always find some point in the combination of result by which those action can be justify by adopt the point of view of a providence who arrange result or of a philosopher who trace they we shall find it possible to obtain perfect complacency in choose to do what be most agreeable to we in the present moment
and it be in this way that philip justify his subtle effort to overcome maggie s true prompt against a concealment that would introduce doubleness into her own mind and might cause new misery to those who have the primary natural claim on she
but there be a surplus of passion in he that make he half independent of justify motif
his longing to see maggie and make an element in her life have in it some of that savage impulse to snatch an offer joy which spring from a life in which the mental and bodily constitution have make pain predominate
he have not his full share in the common good of man he could not even pass muster with the insignificant but must be single out for pity and except from what be a matter of course with other
even to maggie he be an exception it be clear that the thought of his be her lover have never enter her mind
do not think too hardly of philip
ugly and deform people have great need of unusual virtue because they be likely to be extremely uncomfortable without they but the theory that unusual virtue spring by a direct consequence out of personal disadvantage as animal get thick wool in severe climate be perhaps a little overstrained
the temptation of beauty be much dwelt upon but i fancy they only bear the same relation to those of ugliness as the temptation to excess at a feast where the delight be varied for eye and ear as well as palate bear to the temptation that assail the desperation of hunger
do not the hunger tower stand as the type of the utmost trial to what be human in we
philip have never be soothe by that mother s love which flow out to we in the great abundance because our need be great which cling to we the more tenderly because we be the less likely to be winner in the game of life and the sense of his father s affection and indulgence toward he be mar by the keener perception of his father s fault
keep aloof from all practical life as philip have be and by nature half feminine in sensitiveness he have some of the woman s intolerant repulsion toward worldliness and the deliberate pursuit of sensual enjoyment and this one strong natural tie in his life his relation as a son was like an aching limb to he
perhaps there be inevitably something morbid in a human being who be in any way unfavorably except from ordinary condition until the good force have have time to triumph and it have rarely have time for that at two-and-twenty
that force be present in philip in much strength but the sun himself look feeble through the morning mist
chapter iv another love-scene early in the following april nearly a year after that dubious parting you have just witness you may if you like again see maggie enter the red deeps through the group of scotch fir
but it be early afternoon and not evening and the edge of sharpness in the spring air make she draw her large shawl close about she and trip along rather quickly though she look round as usual that she may take in the sight of her beloved tree
there be a more eager inquire look in her eye than there be last june and a smile be hover about her lip as if some playful speech be await the right hearer
the hearer be not long in appear
take back your corinne say maggie draw a book from under her shawl
you be right in tell i she would do i no good but you be wrong in think i should wish to be like she wouldnot you really like to be a tenth muse then maggie say philip look up in her face as we look at a first parting in the cloud that promise we a bright heaven once more
not at all say maggie laugh
the muses be uncomfortable goddess i think oblige always to carry roll and musical instrument about with they
if i carry a harp in this climate you know i must have a green baize cover for it and i should be sure to leave it behind i by mistake you agree with i in not like corinne then i donot finish the book say maggie
as soon as i come to the blond-haired young lady reading in the park i shut it up and determine to read no far
i foresaw that that light-complexion girl would win away all the love from corinne and make her miserable
ibe determined to read no more book where the blond-haired woman carry away all the happiness
i should begin to have a prejudice against they
if you could give i some story now where the dark woman triumph it would restore the balance

chapter vi the hard-won triumph three week later when dorlcote mill be at its prettiest moment in all the year the great chestnut in blossom and the grass all deep and daisied tom tulliver come home to it early than usual in the evening and as he pass over the bridge he look with the old deep-rooted affection at the respectable red brick house which always seem cheerful and invite outside let the room be as bare and the heart as sad as they might inside

ogg s and be the glass of fashion there take some exception to maggie s manner
she have a way of not assent at once to the observation current in good society and of say that she donot know whether those observation be true or not which give she an air of gaucherie and impede the even flow of conversation but it be a fact capable of an amiable interpretation that lady be not the bad dispose toward a new acquaintance of their own sex because she have point of inferiority
and maggie be so entirely without those pretty air of coquetry which have the traditional reputation of drive gentleman to despair that she win some feminine pity for be so ineffective in spite of her beauty
she have not have many advantage poor thing
and it must be admit there be no pretension about she her abruptness and unevenness of manner be plainly the result of she seclude and lowly circumstance
it be only a wonder that there be no tinge of vulgarity about she consider what the rest of poor lucy s relation be--an allusion which always make the miss guests shudder a little
it be not agreeable to think of any connection by marriage with such people as the gleggs and the pullet but it be of no use to contradict stephen when once he have set his mind on anything and certainly there be no possible objection to lucy in herself no one could help like she
she would naturally desire that the miss guests should behave kindly to this cousin of whom she be so fond and stephen would make a great fuss if they be deficient in civility

oggbe for any attention toward she to be neglect
thus maggie be introduce for the first time to the young lady s life and know what it be to get up in the morning without any imperative reason for do one thing more than another
this new sense of leisure and unchecked enjoyment amidst the soft-breathing air and garden-scent of advance spring--amidst the new abundance of music and linger stroll in the sunshine and the delicious dreaminess of glide on the river--could hardly be without some intoxicate effect on she after her year of privation and even in the first week maggie begin to be less haunted by her sad memory and anticipation
life be certainly very pleasant just now it be become very pleasant to dress in the evening and to feel that she be one of the beautiful thing of this spring-time
and there be admire eye always await she now she be no long an unheeded person liable to be chid from whom attention be continually claim and on whom no one feel bind to confer any
it be pleasant too when stephen and lucy be go out riding to sit down at the piano alone and find that the old fitness between her finger and the key remain and revive like a sympathetic kinship not to be wear out by separation to get the tune she have hear the evening before and repeat they again and again until she have find out a way of produce they so as to make they a more pregnant passionate language to she
the mere concord of octave be a delight to maggie and she would often take up a book of study rather than any melody that she might taste more keenly by abstraction the more primitive sensation of interval
not that her enjoyment of music be of the kind that indicate a great specific talent it be rather that her sensibility to the supreme excitement of music be only one form of that passionate sensibility which belong to her whole nature and make her fault and virtue all merge in each other make her affection sometimes an impatient demand but also prevent her vanity from take the form of mere feminine coquetry and device and give it the poetry of ambition
but you have know maggie a long while and need to be tell not her characteristic but her history which be a thing hardly to be predict even from the complete knowledge of characteristic
for the tragedy of our life be not create entirely from within
character say novalis in one of his questionable aphorisms character be destiny but not the whole of our destiny
hamlet prince of denmark be speculative and irresolute and we have a great tragedy in consequence

chapter xii a family party maggie leave her good aunt gritty at the end of the week and go to garum firs to pay her visit to aunt pullet accord to agreement

but then she might be carry very far down and be unable to guide her boat out of the current again

she have pass the mouth of the ripple then now she must use all her skill and power to manage the boat and get it if possible out of the current
she could see now that the bridge be break down she could see the mast of a strand vessel far out over the watery field
but no boat be to be see move on the river such as have be lay hand on be employ in the flooded street
with new resolution maggie seize her oar and stand up again to paddle but the now ebb tide add to the swiftness of the river and she be carry along beyond the bridge
she could hear shout from the window overlook the river as if the people there be call to she
it be not till she have pass on nearly to tofton that she could get the boat clear of the current
then with one yearn look toward her uncle deane s house that lie far down the river she take to both her oar and row with all her might across the watery field back toward the mill
color be begin to awake now and as she approach the dorlcote field she could discern the tint of the tree could see the old scotch fir far to the right and the home chestnuts oh how deep they lie in the water deeper than the tree on this side the hill
and the roof of the mill--where be it
those heavy fragment hurry down the ripple what have they mean
but it be not the house the house stand firm drown up to the first story but still firm or be it break in at the end toward the mill
with pant joy that she be there at last joy that overcome all distress maggie near the front of the house

give i the oar maggie it be not till tom have push off and they be on the wide water he face to face with maggie that the full meaning of what have happen rush upon his mind
it come with so overpower a force it be such a new revelation to his spirit of the depth in life that have lain beyond his vision which he have fancy so keen and clear that he be unable to ask a question
they sit mutely gazing at each other maggie with eye of intense life look out from a weary beat face tom pale with a certain awe and humiliation
thought be busy though the lip be silent and though he could ask no question he guess a story of almost miraculous divinely protect effort
but at last a mist gather over the blue-gray eye and the lip find a word they could utter the old childish magsie maggie could make no answer but a long deep sob of that mysterious wondrous happiness that be one with pain

park house stand high up out of the flood say maggie

some wooden machinery have just give way on one of the wharf and huge fragment be be float along
the sun be rise now and the wide area of watery desolation be spread out in dreadful clearness around they in dreadful clearness float onward the hurrying threaten mass
a large company in a boat that be work its way along under the tofton house observe their danger and shout get out of the current but that could not be do at once and tom look before he see death rush on they
huge fragment cling together in fatal fellowship make one wide mass across the stream
it be come maggie tom say in a deep hoarse voice loose the oar and clasp she
the next instant the boat be no long see upon the water and the huge mass be hurry on in hideous triumph
but soon the keel of the boat reappear a black speck on the golden water
the boat reappear but brother and sister have go down in an embrace never to be part live through again in one supreme moment the day when they have clasp their little hand in love and roam the daisie field together
conclusion nature repair her ravages repair they with her sunshine and with human labor
the desolation work by that flood have leave little visible trace on the face of the earth five year after
the fifth autumn be rich in golden cornstack rise in thick cluster among the distant hedgerow the wharf and warehouse on the floss be busy again with echo of eager voice with hopeful lading and unlading
and every man and woman mention in this history be still live except those whose end we know
nature repair her ravage but not all
the uptorn tree be not root again the part hill be leave scarred if there be a new growth the tree be not the same as the old and the hill underneath their green vesture bear the mark of the past rending
to the eye that have dwell on the past there be no thorough repair
dorlcote mill be rebuild
and dorlcote churchyard--where the brick grave that hold a father whom we know be find with the stone lay prostrate upon it after the flood--have recover all its grassy order and decent quiet
near that brick grave there be a tomb erect very soon after the flood for two body that be find in close embrace and it be visit at different moment by two man who both feel that their keen joy and keenest sorrow be forever bury there
one of they visit the tomb again with a sweet face beside he but that be year after
the other be always solitary
his great companionship be among the tree of the red deeps where the bury joy seem still to hover like a revisit spirit
the tomb bear the name of tom and maggie tulliver and below the name it be written in their death they be not divide
